topic: 政府是否应进一步压低处方药价格？
cont_clean: President Trump has signed an executive order to lower prescription drug prices by tying US medicine costs to lower international prices, aiming to reduce costs by up to 59%. This revives and expands the "most favored nation" policy from his previous term, addressing the fact that US drug prices are nearly three times higher than in 33 other high-income countries according to a 2024 RAND Corporation report. The high costs in the US stem from a complex reimbursement system where pharmaceutical companies set prices negotiated by private insurers, rather than government-negotiated price caps common in other nations. Private insurers typically rely on pharmacy benefit managers (PBMs) with limited transparency, while drug makers defend higher US prices as necessary for funding research and development. While branded drugs are more expensive in the US, the system shows some efficiency with unbranded generic medications, which account for 90% of prescription volumes compared to 41% in other advanced nations. These generics tend to cost American patients about two-thirds of what other countries pay, creating a more nuanced pricing landscape than initially apparent. The new executive order requires drug makers to meet price targets within 30 days and aims to restrict the role of PBMs by creating mechanisms for patients to purchase drugs directly from manufacturers. Though pharma stocks initially rebounded as the measures were perceived as less severe than anticipated and likely to face legal challenges similar to those that blocked previous attempts, regulatory pressure on PBMs continues to grow with bipartisan support for reform. via @cnbc video @vox #drugpricing #prescriptioncosts #trumpexecutiveorder #healthcare #pharmanews #medicationaffordability #healthcarereform #drugcostsavings #pharmaceuticalindustry #healthpolicy #mostfavorednation #pbmreform #medicinecosts #healthcareeconomics #drugpricecomparison #pharmastocks #healthcaresystem #medicationaccess #pbmindustry #prescriptiondrugprices #healthequity #marketregulation #healthcarepolicy #patientaffordability #designed2invest
